title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
author: James Silberman
orientation: An expose contrasting Idaho Rep. Bryan Zollinger's claim that nullification 'doesn't happen in reality' (used to oppose abolishing abortion) with his own previous record of championing nullification bills against the federal NDAA and other federal overreach. Argues that the pro-life establishment's submission to Roe v. Wade has trained politicians to be selectively willing to defy federal authority for every cause except protecting preborn children.

questions.question: What was Rep. Bryan Zollinger's stated reason for opposing the immediate abolition of abortion in Idaho during the HB525 hearing?
questions.answer: Zollinger said the supreme court has the final say on what is constitutional, and the only way around that would be nullification, which he claimed does not happen in practice, so compromise bills like HB525 are the best that can be done.
questions.quote: It's pretty well accepted that the supreme court is the final say of what's constitutional… I don't see any way to get around that unless we went nullification and we all know that doesn't happen in reality.

questions.question: What concrete examples does the article cite to refute the claim that 'nullification doesn't happen in reality'?
questions.answer: The article points to 22 states defying the Federal Controlled Substances Act over marijuana despite the Gonzales v. Raich ruling, plus state and municipal defiance on gun control, immigration, and EPA regulations.
questions.quote: The chances that he's unaware of the 22 states currently defying the Federal Controlled Substances Act which outlaws the possession of marijuana and the *Gonzales v. Raich* supreme court decision which upheld the Federal Controlled Substances Act is very small.

questions.question: How did Idaho HB461, the State Sovereignty Responsibility Act, define the state's relationship to the federal government?
questions.answer: It declared that the states are not bound by unlimited submission to the federal government, that whenever the federal government assumes undelegated power its acts are void, and that Idaho is the final arbiter of whether federal acts are unconstitutional within its borders.
questions.quote: The Idaho Legislature hereby declares that the state of Idaho, on behalf of its citizens, is the final arbiter of whether an act of Congress, a federal regulation or a court decision is unconstitutional and may declare that the federal laws, regulations or court decisions are not authorized by the Constitution of the United States and violate its meaning and intent, and further, are null, void and of no effect regarding any Idaho citizen residing within the borders of the state of Idaho…

questions.question: What did Zollinger himself previously say about Idaho's right to nullify federal laws?
questions.answer: While advocating for HB461 in 2018, Zollinger explicitly affirmed that Idaho has the right to nullify any federal law outside the limited powers granted by the Constitution, and lamented that the state had not exercised that right more often.
questions.quote: We have the right as a state to nullify as a state any law that isn't one of those limited powers. And we haven't exercised that right very often as a state.

questions.question: What did Zollinger's Restoring Constitutional Governance Act (HB473) propose for those enforcing the federal NDAA in Idaho?
questions.answer: It would have nullified the federal National Defense Authorization Act and made anyone attempting to enforce it in Idaho subject to prosecution under Idaho criminal law for offenses including assault, battery, kidnapping, or murder.
questions.quote: Any person who commits a violation of this section shall be prosecuted under the Idaho criminal code relating to the substantive law for which the violation pertains including, but not limited to, assault, battery, kidnapping or murder.

questions.question: How does the article connect Zollinger's behavior to Oklahoma Senate Pro Tem Greg Treat's record?
questions.answer: Treat publicly slammed nullification advocates as 'villains' while opposing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, but he had voted to nullify the Controlled Substances Act for medical marijuana and authored OK SB1379 in 2016 to nullify federal EPA regulations.
questions.quote: But Treat himself voted in favor of nullifying the Controlled Substances Act and *Gonzales v. Raich* to legalize medical marijuana, and not only supported but authored OK SB1379 in 2016 nullifying federal EPA regulations.

questions.question: How does the pro-life establishment frame the supreme court's authority on abortion, according to the article?
questions.answer: Pro-life leaders treat SCOTUS rulings on abortion as effectively binding moral and legal reality, with figures like Joe Pojman comparing defiance of the court to defying gravity and Scott Klusendorf justifying compromise because Roe and Casey have already 'dictated from on high.'
questions.quote: We could no sooner ignore SCOTUS than the force of gravity.

questions.question: What did Abby Johnson say about a Texas bill to abolish abortion, and what does the author claim that implies?
questions.answer: Johnson called The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act unconstitutional, which the author argues amounts to declaring abortion itself constitutional under the current legal regime.
questions.quote: I do not support [The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act] because it is unconstitutional.

questions.question: How does Pastor Bill Ascol explain why pro-life politicians kill abolition bills?
questions.answer: Ascol says these politicians are simply doing what pro-life political organizations have trained them to do, and that the church must stop legitimizing the pro-life industry.
questions.quote: Sadly, when the pro-life politicians kill a bill of abolition, the Oklahoma legislators are doing what the pro-life political organizations have taught them to do. We must stop paying homage to and granting the legitimacy of the pro-life industry.

questions.question: What evidence does the article offer that the paradigm of submission to Roe v. Wade is breaking down?
questions.answer: It cites resolutions passed by the Oklahoma Baptists, Oklahoma Free Will Baptists, and Oklahoma Republican Party in the past year all calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion.
questions.quote: the Oklahoma Baptists (Southern Baptist Convention), Oklahoma Free Will Baptists, and Oklahoma Republican Party all passed resolutions in the past year calling for the total and immediate abolition of abortion.

questions.question: What theological premise does the Free Will Baptist resolution use to ground its call for abolition?
questions.answer: The resolution grounds abolition in the sovereignty of God's law over man's law, declaring that all human life is a sacred gift and that all abortion usurps God's sovereignty.
questions.quote: WHEREAS, God's Word clearly declares that all human life is a sacred gift from our sovereign God and that His Law is supreme over mankind and man's law, and therefore that all abortion is wrong and usurps God's sovereignty

questions.question: What did Rep. Christy Zito say in support of the State Sovereignty Responsibility Act about Idaho's relationship to the federal government?
questions.answer: Zito framed the states as 50 sovereign entities that have entered into a contract with a federal government created to provide certain limited services to the people, and said the bill signaled that Idaho was willing to stand for its citizens.
questions.quote: What this legislation will do is it will send a message that the Legislature of the state of Idaho is willing to stand for its citizens. That the Legislature of the state of Idaho understands that we are 50 sovereign states that have entered into a contract with a federal government that has been provided to provide certain limited services to the people.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: The movement's foundational page on nullification, providing the doctrinal basis the article presupposes.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Directly rebuts Greg Treat's 'villains' framing referenced in this article and defends nullification's historical legacy.

related.reason: Companion piece on Greg Treat's hypocrisy around nullification, exactly mirroring Zollinger's contradiction in Idaho.

related.reason: Profiles the Oklahoma figure the article uses as a parallel case to Zollinger.

related.reason: Another Idaho pro-life politician obstructing abolition, fitting the same pattern documented here.

related.reason: Expands on Joe Pojman's 'force of gravity' quote about SCOTUS that this article cites.
